criteria.label: Explain how a linked list differs from an array in memory layout and complexities.
criteria.example: Array: `[a,b,c,d]` lives in *contiguous* memory; index is O(1) but inserting at position 0 shifts everything (O(n)). Linked list: nodes scattered in memory, each holding a value + a pointer; prepending is O(1) (just relink), but indexing is O(n) (walk from head).

criteria.label: Walk, prepend, and search a singly-linked list correctly.
Use Python's built-ins (deque, OrderedDict, lru_cache) instead of hand-rolled linked lists.
criteria.example: Need an O(1) FIFO for a BFS queue? `from collections import deque; q = deque()` — implemented as a doubly-linked list of blocks under the hood. Don't write your own; the C implementation is faster.

criteria.label: Implement an LRU cache using a hash table + doubly-linked list, both operations O(1).
criteria.example: Combine `dict[key] → node` (O(1) lookup) with a doubly-linked list (O(1) move-to-front, O(1) evict-tail). Or just use `@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=N)` — it's exactly this structure in C.

criteria.label: Detect cycles with Floyd's algorithm; handle delete-with-no-prev edge cases.
criteria.example: Floyd's: `slow = head; fast = head; while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; if slow is fast: return True` — O(n) time, O(1) space. Catches the classic 'tail loops back to a middle node' bug.
